title: Why are Emails Separate from Activies?
excerpt:
I have been trying to get them to address making emails reportable just as activities are and I received this rationale: 
“I’d also like to provide a bit more context on why emails aren’t currently considered activities …

posts.author: jessetolppa (Jesse Tolppa)
posts.cookedHtml:
I have been trying to get them to address making emails reportable just as activities are and I received this rationale:

“I’d also like to provide a bit more context on why emails aren’t currently considered activities in the CRM. Within the CRM platform, there are two types of objects: internal and external.

Internal objects include things like emails and notes. These are considered internal records within the CRM and are mainly used for tracking communication or information that users with the appropriate permissions can view within the system.

On the other hand, activities, such as calls, summaries, meetings, and similar actions, are considered external activities, which represent interactions or actions taken with the contact. Because emails fall under internal objects, they are tracked in the CRM but aren’t automatically categorized as activities in the activity timeline the same way calls or summaries are.”

Does this make sense to anyone? Why can’t they just include Emails as activities or give an option to, at least?

posts.author: Clover_Gal (Desiree McCarthy | monday.com Ambassador)
posts.cookedHtml:
This widget is in Beta and does what you’re looking for? https://support.monday.com/hc/en-us/articles/23905013458450-Activity-tracker-widget

posts.author: Raman_Webguruz (Ramandeep Singh)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hey,

I think the explanation you received actually reflects how monday CRM is currently structured internally, but the confusion is coming from the difference between system architecture and how sales teams use a CRM in practice.

From a data model perspective, what they described makes sense:

  • Internal objects → system records used for documentation and visibility (emails, notes).

  • Activities → structured interaction events (calls, meetings, summaries) that are designed to be tracked and reported on.

So technically, emails are being stored more like communication records, while activities are treated as trackable engagement events.

Where this becomes frustrating for users is that emails are one of the most common forms of customer interaction in sales workflows. In many CRM platforms, emails are automatically categorized as activities because teams want to measure things like:

  • Communication volume per deal or contact

  • Rep engagement with prospects

  • Total touchpoints across the sales cycle

Because of that, it’s natural to expect emails to appear in activity reporting alongside calls and meetings.

So the rationale they gave does make sense from a product architecture standpoint, but the feature gap you’re pointing out is still very valid from a reporting and sales management perspective.

A potential middle ground that would solve this for many teams would be something like:

  • Allowing emails to optionally count as an activity type

  • Adding a reportable email interaction metric

  • Creating an activity log entry automatically when an email is sent or synced

That way, the platform can maintain its internal structure while still allowing teams to analyze email interactions as part of overall CRM activity tracking.

So, the explanation is logically consistent with how their CRM is modeled, but the expectation that emails should be reportable like other customer interactions is completely reasonable.
